messages.text: I began to recover enough to fly the plane home, but the desert did not release me completely. It sent a signal through every star.
At night I looked upward and wondered which light belonged to the little prince. Distance had become both pain and comfort.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: Somewhere a small boy may be laughing beside a rose. Somewhere a sheep may be sleeping inside a box.
Years passed. Adults asked whether my engine trouble had been difficult, and I gave ordinary answers. I did not explain the absence that followed me.
The memory remained sharper than maps: a scarf in the wind, a serious request, a well at dawn, a gentle fall in the sand.
I drew the muzzle later and discovered a terrible mistake. I had forgotten the leather strap. A promise can fail through one small absence.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: Did the sheep eat the rose? Did the prince return in time? I do not know.
No adult answer can settle that question. Some nights the stars laugh clearly, and I feel faith that the rose is safe.
Other nights they sound like tears, and I observe the sky with the fear of someone who forgot a strap.
This is why the desert remains important to me. On a map it is only empty distance, but in memory it hides a well and a child.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: If you travel there and meet a golden-haired boy who asks questions, please be gentle with him.
Do not hurry past. Do not answer like a busy adult. Listen for the wonder beneath the simple request.
If he laughs and refuses to answer your questions, write to me quickly. His return would heal an absence I still carry.
Until then I keep looking. The stars are no longer only stars. They are small bells, distant wells, and a promise still asking to be kept.
The little prince taught me that what we tame continues to call us. Even when invisible, it changes how we see everything visible.
So I watch the sky with a pilot eye and a child heart, hoping the signal of laughter means the rose has survived.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: That hope is my final drawing: a box, a sheep, a rose, and a boy somewhere beyond reach.
Perhaps the drawing is unfinished. Perhaps all love is unfinished, held between faith and fear, asking us to protect what distance hides.
When the night is clear, I choose faith. I let the stars laugh, and for a moment the desert becomes gentle again.
Then I remember his face, and the whole sky feels less empty.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: I have told this story badly if you think it is only sad. It is also full of wonder.
Wonder is why I still look up. A single star can hold absence and comfort together, like a question no adult map can close.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: Sometimes I hear his laughter clearly, and the signal feels like bells tied to every point of light.
At other times memory grows anxious. I picture the sheep, the rose, and the missing strap, and faith must begin again.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: So if you see him, do not let him disappear into polite answers. Tell me he came back.
Until then, every clear night asks me to choose: count the stars like a businessman, or listen to them like a friend.
I leave the question open because love often leaves us with questions. The answer may depend on how carefully we listen tonight.
The story ends there only because my knowledge ends there. His life, if he returned, belongs again to his rose and his star.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: Mine belongs to listening for him.
